title: #architecture: 7088881234
preview:
Nora Blake: Quick sync — I want to propose a compact contract for route lifecycle that reduces ambiguity between policy authors and the runtime. Too many incidents from routes disappearing.
Ethan Park: +1. High level: policy expresses intent; runtime enforces safety. We need explicit fields and a clear clamp/audit m...
text:
Nora Blake: Quick sync — I want to propose a compact contract for route lifecycle that reduces ambiguity between policy authors and the runtime. Too many incidents from routes disappearing.
Ethan Park: +1. High level: policy expresses intent; runtime enforces safety. We need explicit fields and a clear clamp/audit mechanism.
Maya Torres: Suggested minimal data model: `intent_expiry_seconds` (nullable), `soft_deprecate_seconds`, `prune_after_seconds`. Runtime exposes `safety_cap_seconds` per deployment and may shorten any intent but must log `consensus_clamp` with `actor` and `reason`.
Sven Müller: For private/on-prem installs add signed intent bundles. Control plane signs; local admin can sign for air-gapped installs. Runtime validates signature and rejects expired bundles.
Nora Blake: Coordinator question: who schedules prunes? Control plane preferred for hosted customers; local runt...

title: #architecture: 4891234567
preview:
Samir Rao: FYI a customer hit repeated defer loops when the routing policy kept sending retries to the low-cost pool. Saw client timeouts. Need a quick design decision on preventing re-defer loops. :frowning:

Priya Natarajan: Can you share the trace? My initial thought: we need a per-decision marker so retries know...
text:
Samir Rao: FYI a customer hit repeated defer loops when the routing policy kept sending retries to the low-cost pool. Saw client timeouts. Need a quick design decision on preventing re-defer loops. :frowning:

Priya Natarajan: Can you share the trace? My initial thought: we need a per-decision marker so retries know a defer already happened.

Samir Rao: Trace: ```[2026-03-20T13:10:02Z] decision id=9f3de defer_to=pool-low-cost
[2026-03-20T13:10:06Z] client_timeout id=9f3de
[2026-03-20T13:10:06Z] retry parent=abc123 -> decision id=8aa21 defer_to=pool-low-cost
```

Maya Lin: Two policy options: (A) Central policy adds a "defer_window_ms" and runtime honors it; (B) runtime tracks ephemeral token and forbids re-defer for same parent_request_id.

Priya Natarajan: I prefer runtime enforcement with central defaults. Faster, fewer RPCs. But we need token signing for private installs and a tele...

title: #architecture: 4623512345
preview:
Alice Nguyen: Heads up — thinking about consolidating the current "mux" routing adapters into a single multiplexing abstraction. Idea: unify shard-aware, cost-aware, and geo-routing under one minimal API so we can add new heuristics without adapter explosion.

Ben Carter: +1 on consolidation. Can you sketch what the...
text:
Alice Nguyen: Heads up — thinking about consolidating the current "mux" routing adapters into a single multiplexing abstraction. Idea: unify shard-aware, cost-aware, and geo-routing under one minimal API so we can add new heuristics without adapter explosion.

Ben Carter: +1 on consolidation. Can you sketch what the abstraction looks like? worried about performance and lock contention.

Alice Nguyen: rough sketch:
```
mux.Resolve(request) -> [RouteCandidate{id, score, metadata}]
mux.CacheHint(key) -> prefetch/kvcache keys
```
Edge vs coordinator separation: edge does quick heuristic; coordinator runs heavier scoring.

Carla M: @Alice what's the fallback story? we still need per-tenant overrides and canary policy.

Alice Nguyen: Plan:
- minimal runtime API (Resolve/Score/Explain)
- plugin layer for tenant overrides (sandboxed)
- compat-shim for old adapters
- rollout via header: X-Rout...

title: #architecture: 2123456789
preview:
Ava Chen: Quick follow-up from last week's multi-region sync — I'm proposing we add a lightweight 'x-region-stamp' header plus a control-plane residency policy endpoint so edge proxies can do fast local-first routing without holding heavy locks or leases. Thoughts?

Matt Johnson: curious — how is this different from...
text:
Ava Chen: Quick follow-up from last week's multi-region sync — I'm proposing we add a lightweight 'x-region-stamp' header plus a control-plane residency policy endpoint so edge proxies can do fast local-first routing without holding heavy locks or leases. Thoughts?

Matt Johnson: curious — how is this different from our lease swap flow? seems like duplication.

Ava Chen: Good call. The lease swap is still necessary for shard ownership. This stamp is for per-request routing hints for stateless proxies and CDN-like edges where we want best-effort locality and fast fallback. TTL-based, cryptographically signed, no long-lived ownership. :thinking:

Sophia Patel: Love the idea. Example header sketch:
```
x-region-stamp: region=us-west-2; tenant=acme; exp=1712345600; sig=MEFa...
```
Proxies validate sig and expiry, then prefer local pool if policy allows.

Kai Nguyen: How do we prevent some...

title: #architecture: 1829998888
preview:
alex chen: quick follow-up from the RFC — i pushed the "cohesion-by-contract" proposal to the doc (link: https://docs.internal/rfcs/cohesion-by-contract). tl;dr: define minimal runtime contracts between hotpath modules and enforce with adapters rather than big-service splits.

priya natarajan: saw it, thanks. can yo...
text:
alex chen: quick follow-up from the RFC — i pushed the "cohesion-by-contract" proposal to the doc (link: https://docs.internal/rfcs/cohesion-by-contract). tl;dr: define minimal runtime contracts between hotpath modules and enforce with adapters rather than big-service splits.

priya natarajan: saw it, thanks. can you summarize the big tradeoffs? i'm worried about operational complexity if we add an adapter per module.

alex chen: sure — tradeoff bullets: 1) adapters add a small surface but let us keep services cohesive; 2) faster incremental rollouts vs big-cut rewrites; 3) slight latency cost for adapter hop but much lower overall regression risk.

michael ford: do we have perf numbers for the adapter hop? even 1-2ms on the hotpath adds up.

alex chen: good question. rough bench from spike: adapter baseline ~0.6ms median on p3-like nodes, tail depends on serialization. i put the benc...

text:
Claire Dubois: Dropping ADR-019 for review: FP8/INT8 kernel auto-selection heuristics + rollout guardrails. Would like architecture sign-off today so Yuki can land the dispatch changes. Link: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/ARCH/ADR-019-FP8-INT8-kernel-selection-heuristics

Claire Dubois: Key asks:
- Are the selection predicates defensible (GPU arch + shape regime + seq-len split)?
- Are fallbacks/guardrails sufficient to avoid customer-impacting regressions?
- Anything missing re: reproducibility / canary thresholds?

Noah Patel: Read through. Overall direction good, but I want to sanity check two things:
1) “prefer FP8 on H100 for decode-heavy if head_dim in {128, 64}” — we need a hard guard for KV cache alignment + stride; otherwise we’ll rediscover the edge case from earlier.
2) We should be explicit on how we detect “decode-heavy”: is it request-level (observed tokens) or w...

Noah Patel: Dropping ADR here for review: short/long prompt-aware scheduling policy (two-queue + aging) for mixed workloads. Main goal: cut p95 tail without tanking throughput. Please focus on fairness + multi-tenant implications + gating requirements. Link: confluence://architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-short-long-prompt-scheduling-policy

Lena Fischer: tl;dr: classify reqs as short/long based on prompt tokens + predicted prefill cost, keep two queues, dispatch biased toward short w/ token budget + aging so long makes progress. Feature-flagged, defaults off.

Architecture Bot: ADR review opened by Noah Patel. Requested reviewers: @architecture-core @eng-sre

Sofia Ivanova: For context: this is prototype phase only. Not trying to productize tenant QoS yet, but we need to not accidentally create one (or create unfairness that trips up Dedicated customers).

text:
Confluence Bot: ADR updated: ADR-042 Failover Trigger Signal Hierarchy https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-042

Miles Thornton: dropping this here for architecture review. main questions: what signals are authoritative enough to flip regions, and what guardrails we need to avoid oscillation. would love comments today/tomorrow so we can unblock ENG-2412.

Ravi Menon: skimmed. overall +1 on “reachability > 5xx burn > tail latency burn > capacity”. one nit: reachability needs to be *from where*? if it’s only from our control plane vantage points, we risk failing over for a bad ISP / partial internet issue.

Victor Alvarez: yeah, strongly agree. reachability is tricky. i’d recommend defining “authoritative reachability” as: (a) cloud internal (region-to-region) + (b) external synthetic probes from at least 2 providers/PoPs. if only one fails, it’s not authoritative.

title: #architecture: 1768003300
preview:

text:
Talia Benson: Hi folks — need architecture eyes on the Shadow Traffic routing ADR before we mark it “accepted”. Can you review and leave comments inline by EOD tomorrow? Main things we need clarity on: *where* the mirroring happens in the request path + expected perf impact.

Talia Benson: Confluence ADR draft: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-Shadow-Traffic-Routing-Architecture (WIP)

Ravi Menon: Read through. Overall direction makes sense (inline mirroring, fail-open). I think the ADR needs to be explicit about placement:
1) request enters router
2) auth/tenant resolution
3) rollout variant assignment (control)
4) return control response to user
5) mirror *in parallel* to shadow target(s)

Ravi Menon: Right now section 2.1 kind of implies we might mirror before variant assignment; that would make comparing control vs shadow messy.

Mina Haddad: +1 to Ravi. Al...

PR is still WIP but design is pretty stable. @Noah Patel can you take first pass?

Noah Patel: TL;DR: how we ship the hardware tuning profile pack + override precedence (fleet defaults vs per-pool vs per-deploy vs per-route/customer), plus rollback semantics. Would love eyes from Infra/SRE + anyone who’s been burned by config drift.

Ethan Park: skimming now. first nit: the precedence list in section 3 reads like “later wins” but then section 4 implies “fleet defaults override customer overrides for safety”. those are contradictory.

Ethan Park: can we explicitly state: (a) which layers are allowed to override which keys (key-level allowlist), and (b) whether safety clamps are applied after merge. otherwise readers will assume you can override anything via env var.

text:
Claire Dubois: Dropping ADR-0172 draft here for review (metrics + run manifest schema). Would love eyes on: schema fields, versioning, and how we encode workload params + env capture without making it impossible to diff / store. Doc: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/ADR-0172-draft

Claire Dubois: Current proposal is 2 JSON artifacts per run:
1) `run_manifest.json` (inputs + env)
2) `results.json` (metrics + aggregations)

Claire Dubois: Biggest open questions called out in ADR:
- Do we version the schema (semver) vs a date stamp?
- Workload params: flatten vs nested (e.g., sequence length bins / distributions)
- Env capture: how strict is “required” (driver/CUDA, clocks, container digest, git SHA, region, etc.)

Rina Kobayashi: +1 on splitting manifest vs results. For harness it’s way easier to treat manifest as immutable. But we need to be careful that results references man...

title: #architecture: 1762231191
preview:
Ravi Menon: dropping the WIP ADR/design link here for review (enforcement points + policy eval contract). would love eyes esp on trade-offs + auditability: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/hot-route-capacity-protection-adr-wip

Ravi Menon: key question: where do we enforce tier-aware protection? API gateway...
text:
Ravi Menon: dropping the WIP ADR/design link here for review (enforcement points + policy eval contract). would love eyes esp on trade-offs + auditability: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/hot-route-capacity-protection-adr-wip

Ravi Menon: key question: where do we enforce tier-aware protection? API gateway vs routing service vs runtime scheduler (or layered). constraints: low latency overhead, deterministic/explainable decisions for enterprise escalations, no new cascading failure mode.

Ethan Park: from control plane view, I’m leaning layered:
- gateway: static-ish rate limits/quotas + consistent 429 schema
- routing: admission control when we have fleet pressure signals (queue depth / gpu saturation)
- runtime: last-ditch backpressure to avoid total collapse
biggest risk is adding per-request policy network hop.

Vikram Singh: +1 on layered, but we need to be super clear w...

preview:

text:
Rafael Mendes: pulling in #architecture folks for a decision we keep circling. where does the source-of-truth for the public status page component registry live? (component ids, display names, owner, mapping rules, rollout flags). options: repo config vs Confluence page.

Christine Wong: +1. current pain is drift: confluence has “proposed” list, incident-bot has a partial copy, dashboards use slightly diff names.

Irene Choi: repo wins by default for “source of truth” if it’s consumed by tooling. confluence should be a rendered view, not authoritative.

Allison Grant: agree in principle. from observability side: we need the registry to drive dashboard generation + alert labels. we can validate in CI if it’s in git.

Miles Thornton: if it’s in Confluence, the only “validation” is social. also harder to roll back a bad change mid-incident.

Rafael Mendes: concerns I’ve heard:
- oncall n...

title: #architecture: 1753332100
preview:
Karthik Rao: quick design check-in on rollout metadata schema v2. idea: keep runtime wire payload compact, add an optional signed enrichment blob for private installs and for rich clients. want to discuss backwards compatibility, tie-break rules for overlapping pins, and telemetry probe shape.

Zoe Park: love the si...
text:
Karthik Rao: quick design check-in on rollout metadata schema v2. idea: keep runtime wire payload compact, add an optional signed enrichment blob for private installs and for rich clients. want to discuss backwards compatibility, tie-break rules for overlapping pins, and telemetry probe shape.

Zoe Park: love the signed enrichment idea for Private. quick Q: do we need both expires_at ISO and ttl_seconds? i'd prefer single canonical field to avoid conversions in SDKs.

Karthik Rao: agree we should pick one. proposal: canonical on-wire uses ttl_seconds (int) to keep JSON tiny; control plane can surface expires_at in console/ops UI. SDKs can compute expiration if they want human-readable.

Ben Cho: careful: older SDKs may not read ttl_seconds if field name changes. we should include a tiny compatibility hint: "sv" (schema version) integer. older SDKs ignoring unknown fields will be fine;...

text:
Victor Alvarez: Kicking off arch review thread re: VPC endpoints defaults + DNS behavior for the alpha Terraform modules. Main open question: do we enable private DNS on interface endpoints by default, and how does installer validate prerequisites?

Irene Choi: +1. We need something that “just works” for typical enterprise private subnets, but doesn’t silently break when customer has custom DNS / private hosted zones.

Nina Petrova: From the Private installer side: the top failure mode in pilots is ECR pull failing (no endpoint + no NAT) and then people assume it’s “Redwood broken”. We should bias defaults toward endpoints ON.

Marcus Lin: Security angle: prefer endpoints over NAT egress, esp for regulated. But if we do endpoints, we need to be careful about endpoint policies + SG rules so we’re not punching wide holes.

Victor Alvarez: Current proposal (module defaults):
- S3: Gatewa...

1) source of truth: model registry vs control plane DB?
2) public vs internal fields separation (and how hard we enforce)
3) backwards compat / semver semantics for model “versions” vs “variants”
4) deprecation semantics (an...
text:
Quentin Harris: Kicking off schema design thread for model metadata v1. Main open questions:
1) source of truth: model registry vs control plane DB?
2) public vs internal fields separation (and how hard we enforce)
3) backwards compat / semver semantics for model “versions” vs “variants”
4) deprecation semantics (announce vs removal vs EOL) esp for Private bundles

Ethan Park: +1. Control plane is currently the “truth” for serving config (routing, availability), registry is the “truth” for catalog (model family, tokenizer, quant profiles). If we pick two truths we’re gonna keep repeating this project.

Michael Nguyen: Agree. Also reminder: migrations are already scary. If we decide registry is truth, control plane should be read-through + cache and only store serving-state (promotions/rollout state) and maybe some denormalized fields.

Dylan Brooks: From Console POV: we need a single ...

title: #architecture: 1741247710
preview: Connor O'Brien: Kicking off ADR review for Runtime LTS branching/versioning. Main question: do we maintain a dedicated `runtime-1.18-lts` branch (with patches), or do tags-only on mainline (e.g., `v1.18.1-lts` pointing at selected commits)? Looking for eyes on tradeoffs: dependency mgmt, long-term maint cost, and cu...
text:
Connor O'Brien: Kicking off ADR review for Runtime LTS branching/versioning. Main question: do we maintain a dedicated `runtime-1.18-lts` branch (with patches), or do tags-only on mainline (e.g., `v1.18.1-lts` pointing at selected commits)? Looking for eyes on tradeoffs: dependency mgmt, long-term maint cost, and customer pinning semantics (Private/Helm).

Noah Patel: Tags-only feels attractive on paper but I’m worried it collapses the “stable baseline” story. If main keeps moving (scheduler/kernels), tags-only means we’re constantly re-validating a new head for every patch.

Mateo Alvarez: +1. LTS promise is “slower-change”. If we cut from main each month, that’s not LTS, that’s just regular release train with a different label.

Sahana Iyer: From CI/build: tags-only is painful for reproducible builds. We need deterministic toolchain + pinned deps + provenance artifacts. If mainline ...

title: #architecture: 1740018450
preview:
Allison Grant: Dropping the ADR draft here for review: ADR-0127 TP watchdog + abort semantics (WIP) https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/adr-0127 — main open questions are (1) how abort propagates to API layer and (2) retry behavior + drain vs attempt recovery.

Noah Patel: thx. tl;dr from runtime side: if we d...
text:
Allison Grant: Dropping the ADR draft here for review: ADR-0127 TP watchdog + abort semantics (WIP) https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/adr-0127 — main open questions are (1) how abort propagates to API layer and (2) retry behavior + drain vs attempt recovery.

Noah Patel: thx. tl;dr from runtime side: if we declare a TP step stalled, we should assume rank state is unsafe. I’m leaning “drain replica, fail in-flight requests with explicit retryable error, and let router re-place”. In-process recovery is tempting but we’ve been burned by partially wedged NCCL communicators.

Sean Gallagher: +1 to conservative. From incident side, anything that keeps a wedged replica taking traffic is a nightmare (cascades into queue buildup + p99 spikes). Drain/replace is the only thing that actually resolves the symptom.

Rafael Mendes: can we be super crisp in the ADR about *what* is drained? Li...

title: #architecture: 1739993210
preview:
Asha Rao: Heads up: planning a quick EU residency handoff drill next Thursday. Aim: ensure proxy-driven promote works without any non-EU writes. Anyone see problems?

Ben Carter: +1. Clarify: soft-affinity active/active path or emergency promote (active/passive)? We should test both but focus on the promote flow.

A...
text:
Asha Rao: Heads up: planning a quick EU residency handoff drill next Thursday. Aim: ensure proxy-driven promote works without any non-EU writes. Anyone see problems?

Ben Carter: +1. Clarify: soft-affinity active/active path or emergency promote (active/passive)? We should test both but focus on the promote flow.

Asha Rao: Focus on promote flow. Soft-affinity still in place, but during node failure proxy should attempt local standby promote before rejecting writes.

Marta Ruiz: Residency rule recap: tenant writes must stay inside declared region unless explicit export flag set. If promote cannot complete, proxy must return error, not write remotely.

Diego Santos: Technical note: implement a short-lived lease with epoch fencing. Promoter sends fence request to control-plane: bump epoch, get confirmation. If confirmation fails within 20s, reject.

Ben Carter: Agree on epoch fencing. A...

title: #architecture: 1739566123
preview:
Ravi Menon: Kicking off review for ADR-027 (throughput SLO metric definition). Need sign-off from SRE/Obs/Infra/Product before we lock it + route ENG work. Link: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-027-throughput-slo-metric-definition

Ravi Menon: TL;DR proposal: canonical metric = tokens/sec at...
text:
Ravi Menon: Kicking off review for ADR-027 (throughput SLO metric definition). Need sign-off from SRE/Obs/Infra/Product before we lock it + route ENG work. Link: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-027-throughput-slo-metric-definition

Ravi Menon: TL;DR proposal: canonical metric = tokens/sec at the deployment boundary, attributed per `deployment_id` (Dedicated), evaluated over sliding window (not per-request). Requests/sec stays as secondary/supporting metric.

Mina Haddad: +1 to per-deployment. Routing needs an unambiguous unit to protect, otherwise tier-aware admission control becomes guessy. One thing: we should call out whether tokens include prompt+completion (total) or completion only.

Sean Gallagher: Read it. Main concerns:
1) streaming accounting (we already saw double-counting in early metrics),
2) batching/caching: tokens/sec can look “too good” if cac...

title: #architecture: 1739520203
preview:
Ethan Park: need final eyes on ADR before we mark “ready for approval” today. main open item: make the decision points super explicit + document rejected alternatives (blue/green etc). anyone have last-minute concerns?

Talia Benson: +1 please. we keep getting the same questions in review: “why not blue/green” and “...
text:
Ethan Park: need final eyes on ADR before we mark “ready for approval” today. main open item: make the decision points super explicit + document rejected alternatives (blue/green etc). anyone have last-minute concerns?

Talia Benson: +1 please. we keep getting the same questions in review: “why not blue/green” and “what exactly is rollback guaranteed to do.”

Connor O'Brien: read through the draft this morning. i think we need a crisp “Decision” section w/ bullets:
- in-place upgrade driven by generated upgrade plan artifact
- N-1 -> N only initially
- rollback is best-effort but deterministic within contract (and what’s out of scope)

Sean Gallagher: biggest thing for me is we don’t oversell rollback. should say: rollback is guaranteed to restore control-plane + config state to prior version *if* prechecks pass + backup completed + we haven’t crossed non-reversible migration boundary...

title: #architecture: 1739401180
preview:
Ethan Park: pulling folks into this thread from the installer workstream — we need to land rollback semantics + schema migration strategy this week so Connor can cut the next Private train. current ADR draft is here: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/adr-private-upgrade-plan-and-rollback-contract
...
text:
Ethan Park: pulling folks into this thread from the installer workstream — we need to land rollback semantics + schema migration strategy this week so Connor can cut the next Private train. current ADR draft is here: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/adr-private-upgrade-plan-and-rollback-contract

Ethan Park: main open question: what do we *guarantee* on rollback when migrations have run + pods are partially updated? i want something deterministic and enforceable by the installer.

Michael Nguyen: +1. the hard part is DB schema. if we do irreversible migrations (drops, data backfills that change meaning), rollback becomes “restore from backup” only.

Benji Okafor: installer/state machine can do “rollback hooks” but it can’t invent down-migrations. we need to decide contract: either (a) only expand/contract migrations w/ backward compatibility window, or (b) rollback...

title: #architecture: 1739224450
preview:
Miles Thornton: quick write-up from the Incident Bot v2 arch review today. main question: can we ship deploy+config+sat signals without creating a second incident in the process :sweat_smile:

Miles Thornton: tl;dr proposal:
- bot -> diag-aggregator svc (new) -> (a) deploy-metadata svc, (b) config-diff svc, (c) tele...
text:
Miles Thornton: quick write-up from the Incident Bot v2 arch review today. main question: can we ship deploy+config+sat signals without creating a second incident in the process :sweat_smile:

Miles Thornton: tl;dr proposal:
- bot -> diag-aggregator svc (new) -> (a) deploy-metadata svc, (b) config-diff svc, (c) telemetry-query svc
- aggregator does shaping + caching + rate limiting + degraded-mode behavior
- slack UX: 3 cards (Deploy / Config / Saturation) + “Open in Console” links

Rafael Mendes: +1 to putting all fan-out behind aggregator. oncall doesn’t care which upstream is sick, they just want “here’s what we know” and “here’s where to click”.

Logan Wright: biggest risk is telemetry load during real incidents. if #incidents has 40 ppl hammering “refresh context” we’ll DDOS ourselves.

Allison Grant: + paging loops. if bot causes extra latency in metrics pipeline, we’ll get more...

title: #architecture: 1739128800
preview:
Allison Grant: Need a decision today on audit event shape: flattened fields vs nested objects. Main constraint: SIEM compatibility + keep schema stable for v1.

Kevin Osei: +1. From customer reqs: Sentinel + Splunk both *can* take JSON, but nested stuff often ends up as a string blob unless you add parsing rules. Al...
text:
Allison Grant: Need a decision today on audit event shape: flattened fields vs nested objects. Main constraint: SIEM compatibility + keep schema stable for v1.

Kevin Osei: +1. From customer reqs: Sentinel + Splunk both *can* take JSON, but nested stuff often ends up as a string blob unless you add parsing rules. Also dot-notation keys can get weird depending on pipeline.

Logan Wright: For coverage dashboard + queries, flat is easier. Nested means our metrics extraction has to handle multiple shapes (and people will inevitably log arbitrary JSON).

Nadia Rahman: But we still need a way to carry producer-specific details (e.g., policy diff summary, installer operator metadata) without bloating the canonical field list.

Marcus Lin: Security POV: we care about consistent actor/target + correlation first. Anything that breaks queries during an incident is a no.

Ethan Park: Control plan...

title: #architecture: 1738862109
preview:
Miles Thornton: We need to settle backup artifact format stability + migration strategy before we lock docs. Specifically: do we treat backups as a semver’d API? What are our compatibility guarantees?

Michael Nguyen: +1. ENG-9830 is blocked on this. Today the installer emits `formatVersion: 1` in the manifest but i...
text:
Miles Thornton: We need to settle backup artifact format stability + migration strategy before we lock docs. Specifically: do we treat backups as a semver’d API? What are our compatibility guarantees?

Michael Nguyen: +1. ENG-9830 is blocked on this. Today the installer emits `formatVersion: 1` in the manifest but it’s basically “best effort.” If we’re shipping to regulated on-prem, “oops new installer can’t restore old backup” is not acceptable.

Ethan Park: Agree on not breaking restores, but we should be explicit about scope. We’re backing up control-plane state (db + config + some k8s objects) NOT “entire cluster.” That affects what “compatibility” means.

Connor O'Brien: From release side: we can enforce at build time that backup format changes are reviewed (ADR + changelog). But we need a clear policy: what’s MAJOR vs MINOR. Also, we have to support air-gapped bundle verificatio...

title: #architecture: 1738765432
preview:
Priya Natarajan: quick RFC follow-up — thinking about making rollout metadata intentionally minimal on the wire and doing client-delta probing from the console. TL;DR: small pings for compatibility, server-side enrichment for ops. tradeoffs?
Ben Cho: +1 to minimalism, but worried about post-rollback debugging. we sh...
text:
Priya Natarajan: quick RFC follow-up — thinking about making rollout metadata intentionally minimal on the wire and doing client-delta probing from the console. TL;DR: small pings for compatibility, server-side enrichment for ops. tradeoffs?
Ben Cho: +1 to minimalism, but worried about post-rollback debugging. we should keep at least: model, variant, pin_reason, expires_at/soft_ttl. otherwise ops blind.
Mateo Alvarez: +1. also how do we distinguish deprecation vs soft pin? suggest a compact enum like retire_hint: 'none'|'warn'|'soft_retire'|'hard_retire' so clients can surface warnings.
Priya Natarajan: one option. my proposal: wire payload stays tiny, something like:
```json
{"model":"redwood/llama-70b","variant":"q4o","pin_mode":"soft","expires_at":"2026-04-01T12:00:00Z"}
```
control plane enriches with deprecation hints, computed priority, origin, etc., only when a client opts in o...

text:
Talia Benson: Hey folks — need to schedule a design review for the delivery semantics ADR (audit export). This is the one covering at-least-once, idempotency, checkpointing, replay/backfill. Can we get 45m this week? Target attendees: @Ethan Park @Marcus Lin @Kevin Osei @Sean Gallagher @Rafael Mendes @Logan Wright @Jordan Lee. Link: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/adr-audit-export-delivery-semantics-and-idempotency (draft)

Ethan Park: +1. We should lock this before we finalize the control plane config API behavior. I can do Thu 11-12 PT or Fri 2-3 PT.

Marcus Lin: I’m in Thu 11 PT. Main question from Sec side: are we *explicitly* saying at-least-once and pushing dedup downstream? We need crisp language for customers + SOC2 evidence.

Kevin Osei: Same. Also need to be super clear on retention vs export. “Guaranteed delivery” gets interpreted as “exactly-once” and ...

Col...
text:
Jada Williams: Kicking off ADR-034 review re: unit econ cost attribution model. Main open items: allocation basis, idle capacity treatment, and making sure this lines up w/ Finance methodology (Rishi/Laura). Link: https://confluence.redwoodinference.net/display/ARCH/ADR-034-unit-economics-cost-attribution-model

Cole Summers: thx. tl;dr options we captured:
1) allocate compute spend by output tokens
2) allocate by total tokens (in+out)
3) allocate by "effective GPU time" (gpu_ms) from runtime
4) hybrid fallback (gpu_ms where available, else perf coefficient * tokens)

Rishi Malhotra: From FP&A side: we need a definition that reconciles to fleet spend inputs + rate card. Allocation basis matters less than (a) stability + (b) ability to explain deltas. But we cannot have a model that swings purely b/c prompt lengths changed.

Laura Bennett: +1. For exec KPI, we want something we can def...

title: #architecture: 1737324412

Allison Grant: current proposal (rough):
- `event_type`: lower_snake with dot segments: `<domain>.<entity>.<action>`

text:
Allison Grant: dropping the proposed audit event taxonomy naming conventions here for quick review. main goals: consistent, SIEM-friendly, low-cardinality. pls sanity check + call out footguns.

Allison Grant: current proposal (rough):
- `event_type`: lower_snake with dot segments: `<domain>.<entity>.<action>`
- action is verb in present tense (create/update/delete/rotate/enable/disable/export.configure/etc)
- no IDs in `event_type` ever
- avoid logging raw diffs; include controlled summaries

Jordan Lee: +1 on dot segments. just want to make sure we don’t end up with 12 variants of “update”. can we define a small verb set and stick to it?

Allison Grant: yep, verb set is intended to be enumerated. draft list is like: create|update|delete|enable|disable|rotate|revoke|login|logout|impersonate|configure|export|install|upgrade|rollback

Kevin Osei: from SIEM side, dot segments are fine. ...

title: #architecture: 1737052880
preview:
Ethan Park: Kicking off the ADR discussion for optimization config versioning. Core question: do we make configs mutable with an “active” pointer, or immutable versions with promotion/rollback semantics on top? Want to land a decision this week so ENG-4821/4825 can stop thrashing.

Jordan Lee: +1. Also need to be cr...
text:
Ethan Park: Kicking off the ADR discussion for optimization config versioning. Core question: do we make configs mutable with an “active” pointer, or immutable versions with promotion/rollback semantics on top? Want to land a decision this week so ENG-4821/4825 can stop thrashing.

Jordan Lee: +1. Also need to be crisp about what “promotion” means. Is it a state on the config object, or a separate record of “this scope is currently pinned to version X”? Those are very different for audit/RBAC.

Benji Okafor: Option A (mutable config): single config row per scope (tenant/model/region), updates in place. Rollback = write old values back. Fast/simple, but diff history becomes “best effort” unless we snapshot. Also concurrency gets gross.

Michael Nguyen: The “mutable” approach means we basically reinvent versioning via audit table anyway. We’ll need immutable snapshots for diff view + ro...
